book.title: Qañbaq Shal, the Tumbleweed Old Man

chapters.title: The Tumbleweed Old Man
chapters.text:
Long, long ago it was — when the goats were dappled grey and the pheasants were red — there lived an old man named Qañbaq Shal.
He was poor as a bare reed, and alone. He owned nothing but a fishing net, and lived on what the river gave him. He was so thin and light that when the wind rose it rolled him across the steppe like a ball of tumbleweed — and so the people named him Qañbaq.
Each morning his net brought up two little fish. And each morning a sly fox slunk down to the water and took one for herself.
The Fox: “Good morning, Grandfather. Two fish again? How generous. One for you… and one for me. That is only fair — I am so much cleverer than you.”
Qañbaq Shal: “Take it, then, and choke on your fairness. A poor man cannot argue with sharp teeth.”

chapters.title: The Long Road
chapters.text:
Qañbaq Shal: “I cannot bear that fox's mockery another day. I will move on — find another river, another shore, where no thieving fox knows my name.”
He rolled up all he owned, hoisted it onto his thin old back, and set off across the steppe. But the load was heavy and the day was hot, and soon he tired.
So, a little at a time, he hid his things along the way. In one place he buried his bundle of goods. In another he buried the tripe and guts he had gathered from the herders' slaughtered sheep. And in a third he buried a skin of sour ayran, white as snow.
Now — while the load had weighed him down, the wind could not touch him. But the moment the last of it was buried and his back was light again, a great gust swept under him and bowled him over the hills — and set him down in a strange valley.
Qañbaq Shal: “Oh — oh! Here I go — rolling, rolling, like a tumbleweed once more!”

chapters.title: The Valley of the Giant
chapters.text:
He picked himself up, brushed off the dust, and looked around — and his heart nearly stopped. For there stood a GIANT, taking two whole mountains in his hands and striking them together like flint for fire.
The Giant: “Ho! Where are you bound, little old man? Come — let the two of us test our strength!”
Qañbaq Shal: “(to himself) One breath from that fist would scatter me like chaff… I must fight with the only thing I own — my wits.”
The giant lifted a black boulder as big as a cauldron, hurled it high into the sky, caught it in one hand as it fell, and held it out.
The Giant: “There! Now you, old man. Toss it up and catch it — just like that.”

chapters.title: The Sky and the Earth
chapters.text:
The old man wrapped both arms around the boulder. He could not have lifted it an inch. So he looked up at the sky, and down at the stone, and up at the sky again — and stood there frowning all the day long.
The Giant: “Hey! Are you going to stand there forever? Throw it!”
Qañbaq Shal: “I dare not, and here is why. If I throw this stone with all my strength, the sky itself may crack and come tumbling down upon us. And if I fail to catch it, it will punch a hole clean through the middle of the earth. I am only trying to spare us both.”
The giant went pale. He rushed forward and seized the old man's hands.
The Giant: “Grandfather — aksakal — put it down, put it down! Don't throw it! You'll destroy us all for nothing!”
And so the old man learned how shallow the giant's wits ran, and his courage rose. 'Very well,' he said. 'Let us try a gentler game. Let us see who can pull the guts straight out of the earth!'
The giant took a run and stamped with all his weight. The ground caved in to his knee — but nothing came out. Then the old man trotted to the spot where he had buried the sheep's guts, gave the ground a little kick — and with a wet squelch, ropes of steaming guts burst up out of the earth.
The Giant: “You — you tore the guts from the very ground!”
Qañbaq Shal: “Guts are easy. Come — now let us pull out the BRAINS of the earth!”
Again the giant stamped, and again only dirt. But the old man walked to where he had buried the skin of white ayran, kicked once — and up welled a great splash of something white and soft, spilling like brains across the ground.
The Giant: “He pulls the brains from the earth as easily as picking mushrooms… What manner of old man IS this? Aksakal — honoured one — come home with me. Be a guest in the camp of the giants!”

chapters.title: The Camp of the Giants
chapters.text:
So the giant carried the old man to the camp of his kin — a ring of black felt tents each as tall as a hill, where giants sat gnawing whole roasted horses. When the first giant whispered what the little old man had done, the giants drew close together, and their eldest, old Baba Däu, rumbled low.
Baba Däu, the Elder: “If this shrivelled little man can gut the earth and spill its brains, one day he may do the same to us. We will set him tasks no man could do. And tonight, while he sleeps, we will be rid of him. Say nothing. Smile.”
Qañbaq Shal: “(to himself) Smiles by day, and whispers by night. I have sharp ears, my big friends. I heard every word.”

chapters.title: Carry Off the River
chapters.text:
Baba Däu, the Elder: “Guest! Do us one small kindness. Take this water skin to the river and fill it, that we may make tea for our honoured visitor.”
The water skin was a giant's saba — a leather bag so vast the old man could not have dragged it empty, let alone full. So instead he took up a spade and began, slowly and deliberately, to dig a long trench in a great circle all the way around the river itself.
The Giant: “Old man — what ARE you doing? The water is right there!”
Qañbaq Shal: “Fetch a skin today, fetch a skin tomorrow — what a tiresome way to live! No. I will dig the whole river loose and carry it home entire, so you need never fetch water again.”
Baba Däu, the Elder: “Carry off — our whole RIVER?! Stop! Stop, I beg you — you'll leave the camp a dry bone! Give me the skin — I'll fetch the water myself!”
And the great giant snatched up the enormous saba and ran to fill it, sweating, while the old man leaned on his spade and smiled.

chapters.title: Drag Home the Forest
chapters.text:
Baba Däu, the Elder: “Then fetch us firewood, guest, for the night grows cold.”
A young giant tore a whole pine from the earth, roots and all, and swung it onto his shoulder to show his strength. But the old man took a long coil of rope and began, tree by tree, to loop it around the entire forest.
The Young Giant: “Grandfather… why do you tie them all together?”
Qañbaq Shal: “One log at a time? What a waste of an afternoon. I'll drag the whole forest home in a single pull and be done with it.”
The Young Giant: “No — no! Where will we shelter? Where will the birds nest?! Leave the forest be — I'll carry the wood, I'll carry all of it!”
And so the old man carried nothing, and the giants carried everything, and still they grew more afraid of him with every passing hour.

chapters.title: The Night Ambush
chapters.text:
When darkness fell, the giants spread a soft bed for their guest in the largest tent and wished him a smiling good night. But the old man had heard their whispers.
Qañbaq Shal: “I think, tonight, I had best not be where they expect me.”
So he rolled a heavy log beneath the blanket where his body should have lain, tucked it in snug, and crept away to sleep behind the woodpile. At the black middle of the night the giants stole in on tiptoe, lifted a boulder the size of a yurt, and dashed it down upon the bed — crash, again and again — until the log was splinters.
But at dawn, out strolled the old man, stretching and yawning in the morning sun, alive and whole. The giants gaped as though they had seen a ghost.
Qañbaq Shal: “Aah, I slept poorly, friends. In the night some little flea — or a falling leaf, perhaps — kept flicking at my cheek. A trifle. Otherwise, a fine night. My thanks for the soft bed.”
The Giant: “(whispering) A boulder the size of a yurt… and he felt only a flea…”

chapters.title: Gold, and the Way Home
chapters.text:
Baba Däu, the Elder: “Honoured guest — great one — we have kept you far too long from your home. Take this. Take all of it, with our blessing, and go in peace.”
The giants heaped up a great sack of gold and silver — more treasure than the old man had seen in all his days — and, because he was 'too mighty to trouble with a load,' a young giant hoisted the sack himself and carried it all the long way back across the steppe to the old man's little hut.
As they drew near, the old man's small grandchild spotted them from the doorway and came running, hungry and delighted at the sight of the enormous stranger.
The Grandchild: “Grandfather! Grandfather! You've brought us a giant! Are we going to EAT him? I'm so hungry — is it giant-meat for supper tonight?”
Qañbaq Shal: “Patience, little one! Yes — this one looks plump and tender. Sharpen the big knife; we'll feast on giant tonight, and salt the rest for winter.”
The Young Giant: “EAT me?! These little people EAT giants?!”
The young giant flung down the sack of gold, spun on his heel, and fled howling over the hills — and neither he nor any of his kin was ever seen in that country again.
And so Qañbaq Shal — the poor old man so light the wind once rolled him away — carried his gold inside and lived out his long days rich, warm and merry, with his grandchild on his knee. For the strong may shake the mountains, but it is the clever heart, and the kind one, that carries the day.

chapters.title: The Wind's Own Trickster
chapters.text: Qañbaq Shal outwitted a fox, a valley of giants and the black middle of the night — with nothing but his wits — and came home rich. Brain over brawn; the kind and clever heart carries the day.
